notes.note: Cumae, on the bay of Naples, where Tiro was convalescing; Cicero has a villa nearby.
Cicero's villa at Formiae on the coast of Latium, between Rome and Campania, a regular stopping place on his journeys.
Acastus, one of the household slaves who served as couriers in Cicero's correspondence.
Gnaeus Pompeius Magnus, the great general and dynast; here a friendly visitor curious about Cicero's literary work.
The Muses, goddesses of poetry and learning, stand for Cicero's writing; the 'service' is Tiro's secretarial help with his literary projects.

notes.anchor: the true meaning of \textit{fides} --- good faith
notes.note: A play on words: Cicero says he has taught Tiro the 'etymon' (true root sense) of fides, which means both 'good faith / a promise kept' and the strings of a lyre, fitting the talk of music and the Muses.
